This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_21.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

compare_sections.headline_summary: The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of May 1, 2023. The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for services covered by both, while the policy documents do not provide guidance on the billing order between the two programs. The provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on billing order.
compare_sections.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for policy documents. The newest policy documents, `New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10` (effective 2023-05-01), discuss billing instructions for NYS Medicaid FFS and for practitioner administered drugs, but do not specify any scenarios where a pharmacy must bill Medicaid *before* Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:10, 11 — 2023-05-01].
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for provider manual documents, as the guidance explicitly states the opposite. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), mandates that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01]. This establishes a rule that Medicare is the primary payer and must be billed first for covered services, not that Medicaid should be billed before Medicare. This is further supported by the general requirement for providers to 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].
The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.

The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering.
comparison: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].
The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.

The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering.
compare_sections.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide guidance on scenarios where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare. Instead, the provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed *first* for services covered by both programs. Therefore, a direct comparison of rules for billing Medicaid *before* Medicare is not possible with the given context, as no such rules are presented in either source.

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx. Additional information regarding PAD drug policies and billing guidance is
located on the NYS Department of Health (D

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_21.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.

test_id_22.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_22.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

compare_sections.headline_summary: The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of May 1, 2023. The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for services covered by both, while the policy documents do not provide guidance on the billing order between the two programs. The provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on billing order.
compare_sections.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for policy documents. The newest policy documents, `New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10` (effective 2023-05-01), discuss billing instructions for NYS Medicaid FFS and for practitioner administered drugs, but do not specify any scenarios where a pharmacy must bill Medicaid *before* Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:10, 11 — 2023-05-01].
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for provider manual documents, as the guidance explicitly states the opposite. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), mandates that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01]. This establishes a rule that Medicare is the primary payer and must be billed first for covered services, not that Medicaid should be billed before Medicare. This is further supported by the general requirement for providers to 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].
The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.

The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering.
comparison: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].
The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.

The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering.
compare_sections.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide guidance on scenarios where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare. Instead, the provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed *first* for services covered by both programs. Therefore, a direct comparison of rules for billing Medicaid *before* Medicare is not possible with the given context, as no such rules are presented in either source.

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx. Additional information regarding PAD drug policies and billing guidance is
located on the NYS Department of Health (D

requests the item on the date of service;
• a pharmacy submits one course of therapy with no refills; and
• the drug item(s) are dispensed according to:
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines;
NYS laws, rules, and regulations; and
NYS Medicaid Policy.
NYS Medicaid FFS Billing Instructions:
When billing NYS Medicaid FFS, providers should:
1. enter a value of "5" in the Prescription Origin Code field 419-DJ to indicate pharmacy dispensing;
2. enter a value of "99999999" in the Serial Number field 454-EK; and
3. submit the prescriber identification field 411-DB with the dispensing pharmacy’s NPI number.
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Questions and Additional Information:
• NYS Medicaid FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYS Medicaid FFS Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid
Pharmacy Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions should be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Health Plan Contracting and Oversight
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires. For
audit purposes, payment information must be retained for a minimum of six years following the date of payment.
Medicare Part A
Medicare Part A covers inpatient care, including critical access hospitals, and skilled nursing facilities (not custodial or
long-term care). It also covers hospice care and some home health care. Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.
Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary. It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.

compare_sections.headline_summary: The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of May 1, 2023. The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for services covered by both, while the policy documents do not provide guidance on the billing order between the two programs. The provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on billing order.
compare_sections.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for policy documents. The newest policy documents, `New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10` (effective 2023-05-01), discuss billing instructions for NYS Medicaid FFS and for practitioner administered drugs, but do not specify any scenarios where a pharmacy must bill Medicaid *before* Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:10, 11 — 2023-05-01]. An older policy document, `New York State Medicaid Update September 2022 Volume 38 Number 10` (effective 2022-09-01), discusses billing for immunization administration fees and vaccine acquisition costs, also without addressing the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update September 2022 Volume 38 Number 10:3 — 2022-09-01].
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for provider manual documents, as the guidance explicitly states the opposite. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), mandates that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01]. This establishes a rule that Medicare is the primary payer and must be billed first for covered services, not that Medicaid should be billed before Medicare. This is further supported by the general requirement for providers to 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].
The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.

The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering.
comparison: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].
The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.

The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering.
compare_sections.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide guidance on scenarios where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare. Instead, the provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed *first* for services covered by both programs. Therefore, a direct comparison of rules for billing Medicaid *before* Medicare is not possible with the given context, as no such rules are presented in either source.

Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)

State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid

this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.

Manuals/Physician/index.aspx. Additional information regarding PAD drug policies and billing guidance is
located on the NYS Department of Health (D

NYS Medicaid Policy.
NYS Medicaid FFS Billing Instructions:
When billing NYS Medicaid FFS, providers should:
1. enter a value of "5" in the Prescription Origin Code field 419-DJ to indicate pharmacy dispensing;
2. enter a value of "99999999" in the Serial Number field 454-EK; and
3. submit the prescriber identification field 411-DB with the dispensing pharmacy’s NPI number.
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires. For
audit purposes, payment information must be retained for a minimum of six years following the date of payment.
Medicare Part A
Medicare Part A covers inpatient care, including critical access hospitals, and skilled nursing facilities (not custodial or
long-term care). It also covers hospice care and some home health care. Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.
Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary. It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.

test_id_23.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_23.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_23.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

policy.text:
Managed Care (MMC) enrollee specifically
requests the item on the date of service;
• a pharmacy submits one course of therapy with no refills; and
• the drug item(s) are dispensed according to:
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines;
NYS laws, rules, and regulations; and
NYS Medicaid Policy.
NYS Medicaid FFS Billing Instructions:
When billing NYS Medicaid FFS, providers should:
1. enter a value of "5" in the Prescription Origin Code field 419-DJ to indicate pharmacy dispensing;
2. enter a value of "99999999" in the Serial Number field 454-EK; and
3. submit the prescriber identification field 411-DB with the dispensing pharmacy’s NPI number.
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Questions and Additional Information:
• NYS Medicaid FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
• NYS Medicaid FFS Pharmacy coverage and policy questions should be directed to the NYS Medicaid
Pharmacy Policy Unit by telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions should be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Health Plan Contracting and Oversight

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires. For
audit purposes, payment information must be retained for a minimum of six years following the date of payment.
Medicare Part A
Medicare Part A covers inpatient care, including critical access hospitals, and skilled nursing facilities (not custodial or
long-term care). It also covers hospice care and some home health care. Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.
Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary. It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.

test_id_24.answer:
{
   "headline_summary": "The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of May 1, 2023. The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for services covered by both, while the policy documents do not provide guidance on the billing order between the two programs. The provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on billing order.",
   "policy_definition": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for policy documents. The newest policy documents, `New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10` (effective 2023-05-01), discuss billing instructions for NYS Medicaid FFS and for practitioner administered drugs, but do not specify any scenarios where a pharmacy must bill Medicaid *before* Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:10, 11 — 2023-05-01]. An older policy document, `New York State Medicaid Update September 2022 Volume 38 Number 10` (effective 2022-09-01), discusses billing for immunization administration fees and vaccine acquisition costs, also without addressing the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update September 2022 Volume 38 Number 10:3 — 2022-09-01].",
   "provider_manual_definition": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for provider manual documents, as the guidance explicitly states the opposite. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), mandates that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01]. This establishes a rule that Medicare is the primary payer and must be billed first for covered services, not that Medicaid should be billed before Medicare. This is further supported by the general requirement for providers to 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].",
   "similarities": [
      "Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.",
      "Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]."
   ],
   "differences": [
      "The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].",
      "The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.",
      "The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering."
   ],
   "caveats": "Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide guidance on scenarios where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare. Instead, the provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed *first* for services covered by both programs. Therefore, a direct comparison of rules for billing Medicaid *before* Medicare is not possible with the given context, as no such rules are presented in either source."
}
test_id_24.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_24.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_24.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_25.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_25.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

compare_sections.headline_summary: The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of May 1, 2023. The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for services covered by both, while the policy documents do not provide guidance on the billing order between the two programs. The provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on billing order.
compare_sections.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for policy documents. The newest policy documents, `New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10` (effective 2023-05-01), discuss billing instructions for NYS Medicaid FFS and for practitioner administered drugs, but do not specify any scenarios where a pharmacy must bill Medicaid *before* Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:10, 11 — 2023-05-01]. An older policy document, `New York State Medicaid Update September 2022 Volume 38 Number 10` (effective 2022-09-01), discusses billing for immunization administration fees and vaccine acquisition costs, also without addressing the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update September 2022 Volume 38 Number 10:3 — 2022-09-01].
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for provider manual documents, as the guidance explicitly states the opposite. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), mandates that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01]. This establishes a rule that Medicare is the primary payer and must be billed first for covered services, not that Medicaid should be billed before Medicare. This is further supported by the general requirement for providers to 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].
The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.

The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering.
comparison: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].
The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.

The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering.
compare_sections.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide guidance on scenarios where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare. Instead, the provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed *first* for services covered by both programs. Therefore, a direct comparison of rules for billing Medicaid *before* Medicare is not possible with the given context, as no such rules are presented in either source.

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx. Additional information regarding PAD drug policies and billing guidance is
located on the NYS Department of Health (D

process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
