   "headline_summary": "The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of May 1, 2023. The provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for services covered by both, while the policy documents do not provide guidance on the billing order between the two programs. The provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on billing order.",
   "policy_definition": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for policy documents. The newest policy documents, `New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10` (effective 2023-05-01), discuss billing instructions for NYS Medicaid FFS and for practitioner administered drugs, but do not specify any scenarios where a pharmacy must bill Medicaid *before* Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:10, 11 — 2023-05-01]. An older policy document, `New York State Medicaid Update September 2022 Volume 38 Number 10` (effective 2022-09-01), discusses billing for immunization administration fees and vaccine acquisition costs, also without addressing the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update September 2022 Volume 38 Number 10:3 — 2022-09-01].",
   "provider_manual_definition": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare' for provider manual documents, as the guidance explicitly states the opposite. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), mandates that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01]. This establishes a rule that Medicare is the primary payer and must be billed first for covered services, not that Medicaid should be billed before Medicare. This is further supported by the general requirement for providers to 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].",
   "similarities": [
      "Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide any specific scenarios or rules where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare.",
      "Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer or payer of last resort when other coverage (like Medicare) exists. The provider manual explicitly states this for Medicare [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01] and for Third Party Liability generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]."
   ],
   "differences": [
      "The provider manual explicitly states a rule for billing order, mandating that 'Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid' and 'Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01].",
      "The policy documents do not provide any guidance or rules regarding the billing order between Medicaid and Medicare within the provided excerpts.",
      "The provider manual is more recent (effective October 1, 2025) and offers direct guidance on the billing order, whereas the newest policy document (effective May 1, 2023) does not address this specific ordering."
   ],
   "caveats": "Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide guidance on scenarios where a pharmacy *must* bill Medicaid *before* Medicare. Instead, the provider manual explicitly states that Medicare must be billed *first* for services covered by both programs. Therefore, a direct comparison of rules for billing Medicaid *before* Medicare is not possible with the given context, as no such rules are presented in either source."

test_id_25.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_25.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_25.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_26.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_26.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

compare_sections.headline_summary: The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of August 1, 2021. Neither source provides specific requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. The provider manual should receive more weight for current general guidance on prior authorization and billing order, though it does not address the specific query.
compare_sections.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims' for policy documents. The newest policy document, `New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10` (effective 2021-08-01), discusses a 'No-Pay Request for Anticipated Payment (RAP)' for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients, which is required *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, not for prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01]. Older policy documents mention 'pre-approval' for some Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics, Supplies (DMEPOS) items or 'prior authorization (PA) requests' for non-preferred drugs and diabetic supplies, but not in the context of crossover claims or after Medicaid payment [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5, 4 — 2021-03-01].
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims' for provider manual documents. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), outlines various programs requiring prior authorization for certain drugs (e.g., Preferred Drug Program, Brand When Less Than Generic Program) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14 — 2025-10-01]. It also states that providers must 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' and that 'Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit' of a third party to pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. However, these references to prior authorization and third-party liability do not specify requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
similarities: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents explicitly define or provide requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' specifically for crossover claims.

Both sources mention 'prior authorization' or 'pre-approval' in general contexts related to specific drugs or services, indicating that such processes exist within NYS Medicaid, but not for the specific scenario requested.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer, requiring other coverage to be pursued first. The provider manual explicitly states this for Third Party Liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], and policy discusses prior steps for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].
differences: Policy documents mention a 'No-Pay RAP' requirement for Home Health Agencies for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, which is a pre-billing step for Medicare, not prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

The provider manual focuses on prior authorization requirements for specific drug programs and the general principle of pursuing other coverage *before* billing Medicaid, without detailing any post-Medicaid payment prior approval for crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14, 16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (effective August 1, 2021).
comparison: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents explicitly define or provide requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' specifically for crossover claims.

Both sources mention 'prior authorization' or 'pre-approval' in general contexts related to specific drugs or services, indicating that such processes exist within NYS Medicaid, but not for the specific scenario requested.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer, requiring other coverage to be pursued first. The provider manual explicitly states this for Third Party Liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], and policy discusses prior steps for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

Policy documents mention a 'No-Pay RAP' requirement for Home Health Agencies for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, which is a pre-billing step for Medicare, not prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

The provider manual focuses on prior authorization requirements for specific drug programs and the general principle of pursuing other coverage *before* billing Medicaid, without detailing any post-Medicaid payment prior approval for crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14, 16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (effective August 1, 2021).
compare_sections.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide specific guidance or requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' in the context of crossover claims within the provided excerpts. While both mention prior authorization in other contexts, the precise scenario of prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims is not addressed.

policy.text:
August 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

Policy and Billing
New Billing Requirement for Home Health Agencies:
Demand Billing, No-Pay RAP
On January 1, 2021, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) implemented a new Medicare
billing requirement for Home Health Agencies (HHAs) requiring a No-Pay Request for Anticipated Payment
(RAP) for all dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients prior to billing each final claim. The No-Pay RAP must
be submitted within five calendar days after the start of care date for the first 30 day period of care in a 60
day certification period and within five calendar days after the “from date” for the second 30 day period of
care in the 60 day certification period. This CMS billing requirement must be s‐atisfied for all dual-eligible‐
Medicare/Medicaid recipients even if the final claim is not intended to be billed to Medic‐are. Effective
immediately, ‐all HHAs must submit No-Pay RAPs to Medicare for all services rendered to dual-eligible
Medicare/Medicaid recipients.

Medical School (UMass) to perform Home Health Medicare Maximiz
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions. The pharmacist is expected to follow-
up with the prescriber to determine future needs.
Pharmacy Program information is available on the Medicaid Pharmacy Program website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/pharmacy.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Electronic Prior Authorization (ePA)
The Medicaid program accepts electronic prior authorization (ePA) requests via CoverMyMeds. Medicaid enrolled
pharmacy providers who utilize CoverMyMeds can initiate medication ePA requests on behalf of the member for
completion by the prescriber. CoverMyMeds will direct the case to the prescriber’s queue and prompt them to
complete and submit the ePA to NYRx. CoverMyMeds prompts prescribers to answer required clinical questions that
can offer real-time approvals if clinical criteria are met. Medicaid enrolled prescribers can electronically submit prior
authorization requests, upload supporting documents, and track request status in real time. Only prescribers or their
authorized agent may submit a request for a prior authorization. Pharmacists who are not employed by the
prescriber’s office are not authorized agents.
The CoverMyMeds website is available here: Prior Authorization Forms | CoverMyMeds.
For additional information visit the NYRx Education and Outreach (E&O) website or contact the E&O call center. The
E&O Call Center is available by phone at 1-833-967-7310 or by email at NYRxEO@primetherapeutics.com from 8:00
AM to 5:00 PM ET, Monday through Friday, excluding holidays.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_27.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_27.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

compare_sections.headline_summary: The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of August 1, 2021. Neither source provides specific requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. The provider manual should receive more weight for current general guidance on prior authorization and billing order, though it does not address the specific query.
compare_sections.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims' for policy documents. The newest policy document, `New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10` (effective 2021-08-01), discusses a 'No-Pay Request for Anticipated Payment (RAP)' for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients, which is required *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, not for prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01]. Older policy documents mention 'pre-approval' for some Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics, Supplies (DMEPOS) items or 'prior authorization (PA) requests' for non-preferred drugs and diabetic supplies, but not in the context of crossover claims or after Medicaid payment [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5, 4 — 2021-03-01].
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims' for provider manual documents. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), outlines various programs requiring prior authorization for certain drugs (e.g., Preferred Drug Program, Brand When Less Than Generic Program) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14 — 2025-10-01]. It also states that providers must 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' and that 'Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit' of a third party to pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. However, these references to prior authorization and third-party liability do not specify requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
similarities: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents explicitly define or provide requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' specifically for crossover claims.

Both sources mention 'prior authorization' or 'pre-approval' in general contexts related to specific drugs or services, indicating that such processes exist within NYS Medicaid, but not for the specific scenario requested.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer, requiring other coverage to be pursued first. The provider manual explicitly states this for Third Party Liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], and policy discusses prior steps for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].
differences: Policy documents mention a 'No-Pay RAP' requirement for Home Health Agencies for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, which is a pre-billing step for Medicare, not prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

The provider manual focuses on prior authorization requirements for specific drug programs and the general principle of pursuing other coverage *before* billing Medicaid, without detailing any post-Medicaid payment prior approval for crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14, 16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (effective August 1, 2021).
comparison: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents explicitly define or provide requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' specifically for crossover claims.

Both sources mention 'prior authorization' or 'pre-approval' in general contexts related to specific drugs or services, indicating that such processes exist within NYS Medicaid, but not for the specific scenario requested.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer, requiring other coverage to be pursued first. The provider manual explicitly states this for Third Party Liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], and policy discusses prior steps for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

Policy documents mention a 'No-Pay RAP' requirement for Home Health Agencies for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, which is a pre-billing step for Medicare, not prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

The provider manual focuses on prior authorization requirements for specific drug programs and the general principle of pursuing other coverage *before* billing Medicaid, without detailing any post-Medicaid payment prior approval for crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14, 16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (effective August 1, 2021).
compare_sections.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide specific guidance or requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' in the context of crossover claims within the provided excerpts. While both mention prior authorization in other contexts, the precise scenario of prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims is not addressed.

policy.text:
August 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

Policy and Billing
New Billing Requirement for Home Health Agencies:
Demand Billing, No-Pay RAP
On January 1, 2021, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) implemented a new Medicare
billing requirement for Home Health Agencies (HHAs) requiring a No-Pay Request for Anticipated Payment
(RAP) for all dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients prior to billing each final claim. The No-Pay RAP must
be submitted within five calendar days after the start of care date for the first 30 day period of care in a 60
day certification period and within five calendar days after the “from date” for the second 30 day period of
care in the 60 day certification period. This CMS billing requirement must be s‐atisfied for all dual-eligible‐
Medicare/Medicaid recipients even if the final claim is not intended to be billed to Medic‐are. Effective
immediately, ‐all HHAs must submit No-Pay RAPs to Medicare for all services rendered to dual-eligible
Medicare/Medicaid recipients.
The Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG) has contracted with the University of Massachusetts
Medical School (UMass) to perform Home Health Medicare Maximiz
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions. The pharmacist is expected to follow-
up with the prescriber to determine future needs.
Pharmacy Program information is available on the Medicaid Pharmacy Program website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/pharmacy.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Electronic Prior Authorization (ePA)
The Medicaid program accepts electronic prior authorization (ePA) requests via CoverMyMeds. Medicaid enrolled
pharmacy providers who utilize CoverMyMeds can initiate medication ePA requests on behalf of the member for
completion by the prescriber. CoverMyMeds will direct the case to the prescriber’s queue and prompt them to
complete and submit the ePA to NYRx. CoverMyMeds prompts prescribers to answer required clinical questions that
can offer real-time approvals if clinical criteria are met. Medicaid enrolled prescribers can electronically submit prior
authorization requests, upload supporting documents, and track request status in real time. Only prescribers or their
authorized agent may submit a request for a prior authorization. Pharmacists who are not employed by the
prescriber’s office are not authorized agents.
The CoverMyMeds website is available here: Prior Authorization Forms | CoverMyMeds.
For additional information visit the NYRx Education and Outreach (E&O) website or contact the E&O call center. The
E&O Call Center is available by phone at 1-833-967-7310 or by email at NYRxEO@primetherapeutics.com from 8:00
AM to 5:00 PM ET, Monday through Friday, excluding holidays.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_27.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_27.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_27.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions. The pharmacist is expected to follow-
up with the prescriber to determine future needs.
Pharmacy Program information is available on the Medicaid Pharmacy Program website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/pharmacy.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Electronic Prior Authorization (ePA)
The Medicaid program accepts electronic prior authorization (ePA) requests via CoverMyMeds. Medicaid enrolled
pharmacy providers who utilize CoverMyMeds can initiate medication ePA requests on behalf of the member for
completion by the prescriber. CoverMyMeds will direct the case to the prescriber’s queue and prompt them to
complete and submit the ePA to NYRx. CoverMyMeds prompts prescribers to answer required clinical questions that
can offer real-time approvals if clinical criteria are met. Medicaid enrolled prescribers can electronically submit prior
authorization requests, upload supporting documents, and track request status in real time. Only prescribers or their
authorized agent may submit a request for a prior authorization. Pharmacists who are not employed by the
prescriber’s office are not authorized agents.
The CoverMyMeds website is available here: Prior Authorization Forms | CoverMyMeds.
For additional information visit the NYRx Education and Outreach (E&O) website or contact the E&O call center. The
E&O Call Center is available by phone at 1-833-967-7310 or by email at NYRxEO@primetherapeutics.com from 8:00
AM to 5:00 PM ET, Monday through Friday, excluding holidays.

test_id_28.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_28.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_28.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_29.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_29.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

compare_sections.headline_summary: The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of August 1, 2021. Neither source provides specific requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. The provider manual should receive more weight for current general guidance on prior authorization and billing order, though it does not address the specific query.
compare_sections.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims' for policy documents. The newest policy document, `New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10` (effective 2021-08-01), discusses a 'No-Pay Request for Anticipated Payment (RAP)' for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients, which is required *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, not for prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01]. Older policy documents mention 'pre-approval' for some Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics, Supplies (DMEPOS) items or 'prior authorization (PA) requests' for non-preferred drugs and diabetic supplies, but not in the context of crossover claims or after Medicaid payment [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5, 4 — 2021-03-01].
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims' for provider manual documents. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), outlines various programs requiring prior authorization for certain drugs (e.g., Preferred Drug Program, Brand When Less Than Generic Program) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14 — 2025-10-01]. It also states that providers must 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' and that 'Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit' of a third party to pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. However, these references to prior authorization and third-party liability do not specify requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
similarities: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents explicitly define or provide requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' specifically for crossover claims.

Both sources mention 'prior authorization' or 'pre-approval' in general contexts related to specific drugs or services, indicating that such processes exist within NYS Medicaid, but not for the specific scenario requested.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer, requiring other coverage to be pursued first. The provider manual explicitly states this for Third Party Liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], and policy discusses prior steps for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].
differences: Policy documents mention a 'No-Pay RAP' requirement for Home Health Agencies for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, which is a pre-billing step for Medicare, not prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

The provider manual focuses on prior authorization requirements for specific drug programs and the general principle of pursuing other coverage *before* billing Medicaid, without detailing any post-Medicaid payment prior approval for crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14, 16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (effective August 1, 2021).
comparison: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents explicitly define or provide requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' specifically for crossover claims.

Both sources mention 'prior authorization' or 'pre-approval' in general contexts related to specific drugs or services, indicating that such processes exist within NYS Medicaid, but not for the specific scenario requested.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer, requiring other coverage to be pursued first. The provider manual explicitly states this for Third Party Liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], and policy discusses prior steps for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

Policy documents mention a 'No-Pay RAP' requirement for Home Health Agencies for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, which is a pre-billing step for Medicare, not prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

The provider manual focuses on prior authorization requirements for specific drug programs and the general principle of pursuing other coverage *before* billing Medicaid, without detailing any post-Medicaid payment prior approval for crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14, 16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (effective August 1, 2021).
compare_sections.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide specific guidance or requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' in the context of crossover claims within the provided excerpts. While both mention prior authorization in other contexts, the precise scenario of prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims is not addressed.

policy.text:
August 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

Policy and Billing
New Billing Requirement for Home Health Agencies:
Demand Billing, No-Pay RAP
On January 1, 2021, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) implemented a new Medicare
billing requirement for Home Health Agencies (HHAs) requiring a No-Pay Request for Anticipated Payment
(RAP) for all dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients prior to billing each final claim. The No-Pay RAP must
be submitted within five calendar days after the start of care date for the first 30 day period of care in a 60
day certification period and within five calendar days after the “from date” for the second 30 day period of
care in the 60 day certification period. This CMS billing requirement must be s‐atisfied for all dual-eligible‐
Medicare/Medicaid recipients even if the final claim is not intended to be billed to Medic‐are. Effective
immediately, ‐all HHAs must submit No-Pay RAPs to Medicare for all services rendered to dual-eligible
Medicare/Medicaid recipients.
The Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG) has contracted with the University of Massachusetts
Medical School (UMass) to perform Home Health Medicare Maximiz

      "The provider manual focuses on prior authorization requirements for specific drug programs and the general principle of pursuing other coverage *before* billing Medicaid, without detailing any post-Medicaid payment prior approval for crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14, 16, 17 — 2025-10-01].",
      "The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (effective August 1, 2021)."
   ],
   "caveats": "Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide specific guidance or requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' in the context of crossover claims within the provided excerpts. While both mention prior authorization in other contexts, the precise scenario of prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims is not addressed."
}
test_id_29.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_29.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_29.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_30.question: For crossover claims, what are the requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment?
test_id_30.concept: crossover claims prior approval after Medicaid payment

compare_sections.headline_summary: The provider manual documents, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, are more recent than the policy documents, which have a newest effective date of August 1, 2021. Neither source provides specific requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims. The provider manual should receive more weight for current general guidance on prior authorization and billing order, though it does not address the specific query.
compare_sections.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims' for policy documents. The newest policy document, `New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10` (effective 2021-08-01), discusses a 'No-Pay Request for Anticipated Payment (RAP)' for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients, which is required *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, not for prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01]. Older policy documents mention 'pre-approval' for some Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics, Supplies (DMEPOS) items or 'prior authorization (PA) requests' for non-preferred drugs and diabetic supplies, but not in the context of crossover claims or after Medicaid payment [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5, 4 — 2021-03-01].
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define 'requirements for prior approval after Medicaid payment for crossover claims' for provider manual documents. The newest provider manual document, `New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines` (effective 2025-10-01), outlines various programs requiring prior authorization for certain drugs (e.g., Preferred Drug Program, Brand When Less Than Generic Program) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14 — 2025-10-01]. It also states that providers must 'pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid' and that 'Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibilit' of a third party to pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]. However, these references to prior authorization and third-party liability do not specify requirements for prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims.
similarities: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents explicitly define or provide requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' specifically for crossover claims.

Both sources mention 'prior authorization' or 'pre-approval' in general contexts related to specific drugs or services, indicating that such processes exist within NYS Medicaid, but not for the specific scenario requested.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer, requiring other coverage to be pursued first. The provider manual explicitly states this for Third Party Liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], and policy discusses prior steps for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].
differences: Policy documents mention a 'No-Pay RAP' requirement for Home Health Agencies for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, which is a pre-billing step for Medicare, not prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

The provider manual focuses on prior authorization requirements for specific drug programs and the general principle of pursuing other coverage *before* billing Medicaid, without detailing any post-Medicaid payment prior approval for crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14, 16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (effective August 1, 2021).
comparison: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents explicitly define or provide requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' specifically for crossover claims.

Both sources mention 'prior authorization' or 'pre-approval' in general contexts related to specific drugs or services, indicating that such processes exist within NYS Medicaid, but not for the specific scenario requested.

Both sources, by implication or explicit statement, position Medicaid as a secondary payer, requiring other coverage to be pursued first. The provider manual explicitly states this for Third Party Liability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01], and policy discusses prior steps for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

Policy documents mention a 'No-Pay RAP' requirement for Home Health Agencies for dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients *prior to billing each final claim* to Medicare, which is a pre-billing step for Medicare, not prior approval after Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:14, 15 — 2021-08-01].

The provider manual focuses on prior authorization requirements for specific drug programs and the general principle of pursuing other coverage *before* billing Medicaid, without detailing any post-Medicaid payment prior approval for crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:14, 16, 17 — 2025-10-01].
The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective October 1, 2025) compared to the newest policy document (effective August 1, 2021).
compare_sections.caveats: Neither the policy documents nor the provider manual documents provide specific guidance or requirements for 'prior approval after Medicaid payment' in the context of crossover claims within the provided excerpts. While both mention prior authorization in other contexts, the precise scenario of prior approval *after* Medicaid payment for crossover claims is not addressed.

policy.text:
August 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

Policy and Billing
New Billing Requirement for Home Health Agencies:
Demand Billing, No-Pay RAP
On January 1, 2021, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) implemented a new Medicare
billing requirement for Home Health Agencies (HHAs) requiring a No-Pay Request for Anticipated Payment
(RAP) for all dual-eligible Medicare/Medicaid recipients prior to billing each final claim. The No-Pay RAP must
be submitted within five calendar days after the start of care date for the first 30 day period of care in a 60
day certification period and within five calendar days after the “from date” for the second 30 day period of
care in the 60 day certification period. This CMS billing requirement must be s‐atisfied for all dual-eligible‐
Medicare/Medicaid recipients even if the final claim is not intended to be billed to Medic‐are. Effective
immediately, ‐all HHAs must submit No-Pay RAPs to Medicare for all services rendered to dual-eligible
Medicare/Medicaid recipients.
The Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG) has contracted with the University of Massachusetts
Medical School (UMass) to perform Home Health Medicare Maximiz

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions. The pharmacist is expected to follow-
up with the prescriber to determine future needs.
Pharmacy Program information is available on the Medicaid Pharmacy Program website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/pharmacy.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Electronic Prior Authorization (ePA)
The Medicaid program accepts electronic prior authorization (ePA) requests via CoverMyMeds. Medicaid enrolled
pharmacy providers who utilize CoverMyMeds can initiate medication ePA requests on behalf of the member for
completion by the prescriber. CoverMyMeds will direct the case to the prescriber’s queue and prompt them to
complete and submit the ePA to NYRx. CoverMyMeds prompts prescribers to answer required clinical questions that
can offer real-time approvals if clinical criteria are met. Medicaid enrolled prescribers can electronically submit prior
authorization requests, upload supporting documents, and track request status in real time. Only prescribers or their
authorized agent may submit a request for a prior authorization. Pharmacists who are not employed by the
prescriber’s office are not authorized agents.
The CoverMyMeds website is available here: Prior Authorization Forms | CoverMyMeds.
For additional information visit the NYRx Education and Outreach (E&O) website or contact the E&O call center. The
E&O Call Center is available by phone at 1-833-967-7310 or by email at NYRxEO@primetherapeutics.com from 8:00
AM to 5:00 PM ET, Monday through Friday, excluding holidays.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
